items.question_text: When tower positions are combined, which frequency must not be used for airborne communications?

choices.text: Ground control frequency.
Local control frequency.
The frequency named in the ATIS.
Any frequency shared with clearance delivery.

items.explanation: When tower positions are combined, airborne communications are not conducted on ground control frequency.

items.situation: A tower is combining local and ground positions. Another controller suggests putting an inbound aircraft on ground frequency to keep the local frequency less congested.
items.para_context: When positions are combined in the tower, do not use ground control frequency for airborne communications.

choices.text: Keep airborne communications off the ground control frequency and work the aircraft on the frequency intended for that airborne function.
Move the aircraft to ground frequency because once positions are combined the distinction between local and ground no longer matters.
Tell the pilot to monitor both frequencies and answer whichever controller calls first.
Use ground frequency only if the aircraft is within the traffic pattern, since it is still close to the airport.

items.explanation: The rule is about the purpose of the frequency, not just the pilot's proximity to the airport. Airborne communications stay off ground control frequency.

items.question_text: When local and ground functions are combined on one frequency, what setup is used and how may ATIS help?

choices.text: Combine them on local control, and use ATIS to specify the desired frequency.
Combine them on ground control, and use ATIS only to announce that frequencies are split.
Leave both frequencies active and use ATIS to tell pilots to choose the less busy one.
Use clearance delivery as the shared frequency, and use ATIS only for weather.

items.explanation: The note recommends combining on local control, and ATIS may be used to specify the desired frequency.

items.question_text: What is the normal monitoring requirement for interphones and assigned radio frequencies?

choices.text: Monitor them continuously.
Monitor them whenever traffic volume becomes moderate or higher.
Monitor interphones continuously, but assigned radio frequencies only when expected traffic is IFR.
Monitor only the primary assigned frequency unless a supervisor directs otherwise.

items.explanation: Paragraph 2-4-2 states the continuous-monitoring requirement directly.

items.question_text: Which facilities are specifically identified in the note as required to monitor all assigned frequencies continuously?

choices.text: All FAA facilities, including RAPCONs and RATCFs.
Only ARTCCs and approach controls with more than one radar position open.
Only control towers, because interphone monitoring is implied for all other facilities.
Only facilities using published frequencies.

items.explanation: The note explicitly says all FAA facilities, including RAPCONs and RATCFs, are required to monitor all assigned frequencies continuously.

items.situation: Traffic has been quiet for several minutes, and a controller at an FAA facility wants to stop monitoring a less-used assigned frequency until traffic picks up again.
items.para_context: Monitor interphones and assigned radio frequencies continuously.

choices.text: Keep monitoring the assigned frequency continuously.
Stop monitoring it temporarily, because continuous monitoring applies only when the frequency is actively in use.
Switch it to guard instead, because guard monitoring can substitute for assigned-frequency monitoring.
Monitor it only every five minutes as long as the interphone remains monitored continuously.

items.explanation: At FAA facilities, assigned radio frequencies are continuously monitored, not only when they are busy.

items.instruction: The controller issued this instruction. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: HOLD SHORT OF RUNWAY ONE EIGHT.

choices.text: Hold short of Runway One Eight, Cessna Three Alpha Bravo.
Roger, Cessna Three Alpha Bravo.
Hold short of Runway One Seven, Cessna Three Alpha Bravo.
Cross Runway One Eight, Cessna Three Alpha Bravo.

items.explanation: Hold short instructions require an accurate readback, and the acknowledgment must identify the aircraft.

items.situation: A pilot reads back a taxi clearance but omits the hold short instruction and does not identify the aircraft in the acknowledgment.
items.para_context: Ensure items read back are correct, ensure the read back of hold short instructions, and ensure pilots use call signs or registration numbers in any read back acknowledging a clearance or instruction.

choices.text: Obtain a correct readback of the hold short instruction and ensure the acknowledgment includes the call sign or registration.
Accept the acknowledgment because any response shows the pilot heard the instruction.
Correct only the aircraft identification and ignore the missing hold short readback.
Wait to see whether the aircraft stops before deciding whether the readback matters.

items.explanation: The controller must ensure the hold short instruction is read back and that the acknowledgment identifies the aircraft.

items.question_text: What should the initial readback of a taxi, departure, or landing clearance include?

choices.text: The runway assignment, including left, right, or center when applicable.
Only the altitude or route numbers, because runway assignment is already understood from the context.
The word wilco instead of the runway assignment unless the controller asks for the numbers.
The facility identification before the runway assignment.

items.explanation: The note under 2-4-3 says the initial readback of a taxi, departure, or landing clearance should include the runway assignment.

items.question_text: When may a pilot be authorized to interrupt communications guard?

choices.text: As necessary.
Only after declaring an emergency.
Only after landing clearance has been issued.
Only when the pilot is outside controlled airspace.

items.explanation: Paragraph 2-4-4 authorizes interruption of communications guard as necessary.

items.situation: A pilot with only one operative communications radio has a safety-related company issue and requests permission to abandon guard for a mutually agreeable period while remaining in contact with ATC on the assigned frequency.
items.para_context: As necessary, authorize a pilot to interrupt communications guard. In the one-radio safety example, the pilot advises ATC which NAVAID voice facility and company frequency will be monitored.

choices.text: Authorize the interruption as necessary and ensure the pilot provides the NAVAID voice facility and company frequency to be monitored.
Deny the request automatically, because guard may never be interrupted once an aircraft is airborne.
Tell the pilot to leave the assigned ATC frequency instead of guard because ATC can relay any company information later.
Approve the interruption only if the pilot agrees not to report which company frequency will be monitored.

items.explanation: The note describes this exact one-radio, safety-related use case and the information the pilot provides.

items.question_text: If a pilot interrupts guard under the note to 2-4-4, what information should the pilot provide to controllers?

choices.text: The NAVAID voice facility and the company frequency the pilot will monitor.
Only the expected duration, because the monitored frequencies are private company information.
Only the reason for the interruption, because the monitored frequency does not affect ATC.
The destination airport and the aircraft's filed alternate.

items.explanation: The note says the pilot informs controllers of both the NAVAID voice facility and the company frequency to be monitored.

items.question_text: Which transmissions are authorized on ATC communications channels?

choices.text: Only messages necessary for air traffic control or otherwise contributing to air safety.
Any operationally interesting information, even if it does not affect ATC or safety.
Routine company messages whenever the frequency is quiet.
Administrative traffic if all controllers on the position agree.

items.explanation: The paragraph limits transmissions to ATC necessity or air-safety contribution.

items.situation: The control frequency is quiet. An airline asks you to pass a routine gate-assignment update to one of its aircraft because dispatch cannot reach the crew another way.
items.para_context: Transmit only those messages necessary for air traffic control or otherwise contributing to air safety.

choices.text: Do not pass it on ATC frequency unless it is necessary for ATC or contributes to air safety.
Pass it because any quiet period on the frequency allows non-ATC company traffic.
Pass it only if the crew first says unable to contact company on another channel.
Pass it because anything involving an aircraft operator automatically counts as ATC business.

items.explanation: Frequency quietness is not the standard. Necessity for ATC or air safety is.

items.question_text: May a message that is not directly part of control instructions still be transmitted if it affects safe flight operations?

choices.text: It may be transmitted if it contributes to air safety.
It may not be transmitted unless it is worded as a clearance.
It may be transmitted only after the aircraft leaves controlled airspace.
It may be transmitted only if the pilot specifically requests it by call sign.

items.explanation: The paragraph expressly allows messages that otherwise contribute to air safety.

items.question_text: Which response best matches the required actions when false or deceptive communications occur?

choices.text: Correct the false information, alert aircraft in the affected area, collect pertinent information, and notify the operations supervisor.
Wait for a second false transmission before broadcasting anything so you do not create unnecessary alarm.
Change only the working frequency and continue normal operations unless a pilot reports confusion.
Tell aircraft to ignore all ATC instructions until the source is located.

items.explanation: The paragraph lays out a four-part response: correct, broadcast, collect, and notify.

items.instruction: Put the false-communication response steps in order.

steps.text: Broadcast an alert to aircraft operating on all frequencies within the affected area.
Collect pertinent information regarding the incident.
Notify the operations supervisor and report all relevant information pertaining to the incident.

items.explanation: The required flow in 2-4-6 is to correct first, then alert aircraft, then collect information, then notify the operations supervisor.

items.situation: False ATC instructions have been reported near Long Beach Airport. Build the warning broadcast.
items.target_phrase: ATTENTION ALL AIRCRAFT FALSE ATC INSTRUCTIONS HAVE BEEN RECEIVED IN THE AREA OF LONG BEACH AIRPORT EXERCISE EXTREME CAUTION ON ALL FREQUENCIES AND VERIFY INSTRUCTIONS

items.question_text: When relaying operational information to an aircraft or aircraft operator, what two limitations apply?

choices.text: Give the source of the message and do not agree to handle such relays on a regular basis.
Give the source only if asked, and handle the relays regularly once the operator has validated them.
Treat the message as your own and avoid identifying the source to reduce confusion.
Relay it only if it comes from another ATC facility and never if it comes from an operator.

items.explanation: Necessary relays are allowed, but the source must be given and regular handling should not be accepted.

items.situation: An airline repeatedly asks your position to pass routine operational messages to one of its aircraft whenever dispatch is busy. The controller taking the request wants to just make it a normal service the position provides.
items.para_context: Relay operational information as necessary, do not agree to handle such messages on a regular basis, and give the source of any such message you relay.

choices.text: Relay only as necessary, give the source, and do not agree to handle the messages on a regular basis.
Agree to make it a standard service because the messages are operational and the airline uses your airspace often.
Refuse every relay request, because controllers may never relay operator-originated information.
Relay the message but remove the source so the flight crew treats it as direct ATC guidance.

items.explanation: The paragraph permits necessary relays but expressly forbids agreeing to handle them routinely.

items.question_text: Which additional relay categories may be relayed in addition to as-needed operational information for aircraft or operators?

choices.text: Official FAA messages as required, and operational information to military aircraft operating on or planning to operate on IRs.
Only military messages, because official FAA messages must always go by administrative channels.
Only official FAA messages, because IR-related traffic is handled entirely by flight service.
Neither of those; 2-4-7 is limited strictly to civil operator traffic.

items.explanation: Subparagraphs b and c identify those additional relay categories directly.

items.question_text: Which sequence matches the standard format for initial radio contact with an aircraft?

choices.text: Aircraft identification, ATC unit identification, message if any, and "over" if required.
ATC unit identification, aircraft identification, message if any, and "over" if required.
Message if any, aircraft identification, ATC unit identification, and "over" if required.
Aircraft identification, message if any, ATC unit identification, and "over" if required.

items.explanation: The initial format is listed in that order.

items.instruction: Put the initial radio-call elements in order.
items.explanation: Initial radio contact uses aircraft ID first, then ATC unit ID, then any message, then "over" if required.

items.question_text: On subsequent radio transmissions from the same sector or position, what may be omitted from the format?

choices.text: Use the same format, except the identification of the ATC unit may be omitted.
Use only the message, because the aircraft already knows both the aircraft and unit identifications.
Omit the aircraft identification but always keep the ATC unit identification.
Reverse the order so the ATC unit identification comes before the aircraft identification.

items.explanation: Subsequent transmissions keep the same structure except that the ATC unit identification may be omitted.

items.question_text: When tower positions are combined, which frequency must not be used for airborne communications?
items.explanation: When tower positions are combined, airborne communications are not conducted on ground control frequency.

choices.text: Ground control frequency.
Local control frequency.
ATIS frequency.
Clearance delivery frequency.

items.question_text: Why does 2-4-1 warn against airborne use of a ground control frequency when positions are combined?
items.explanation: The note explains that airborne use of a ground control frequency can create interference to or from other towers.

choices.text: It can cause interference to other towers or interference to your aircraft from another tower.
It prevents ATIS from transmitting weather updates.
It makes all surface-movement instructions invalid until repeated on local control.
It blocks use of the tower light-gun system.

items.question_text: When local and ground functions are combined, what frequency setup is recommended and how may ATIS support it?
items.explanation: The note recommends combining the functions on local control, and ATIS may be used to specify the desired frequency.

choices.text: Combine them on local control and use ATIS to specify the desired frequency.
Combine them on ground control and use ATIS only for taxi-route changes.
Keep both frequencies active and use ATIS to tell pilots to pick one at random.
Move both functions to clearance delivery and use ATIS only for weather dissemination.

items.question_text: What must be monitored continuously under 2-4-2?
items.explanation: The paragraph requires continuous monitoring of interphones and assigned radio frequencies.

choices.text: Interphones and assigned radio frequencies.
Only primary frequencies that currently have traffic.
Interphones only, unless the supervisor reassigns a radio frequency.
Only published frequencies, not discrete ones.

items.question_text: Which facilities are specifically named in the 2-4-2 note as required to monitor all assigned frequencies continuously?
items.explanation: The note names all FAA facilities, including RAPCONs and RATCFs.

choices.text: All FAA facilities, including RAPCONs and RATCFs.
Only towers and terminal radar facilities.
Only ARTCCs that own the frequencies locally.
Only military-operated FAA contract facilities.

items.question_text: What caveat does the note to 2-4-2 make for USAF facilities?
items.explanation: The note says USAF facilities may not monitor all unpublished discrete frequencies.

choices.text: They may not monitor all unpublished discrete frequencies.
They are exempt from monitoring interphones continuously.
They may monitor only company frequencies when traffic is light.
They are prohibited from monitoring any unpublished frequency at all.

items.question_text: Which readback must controllers specifically ensure under 2-4-3?
items.explanation: Hold short instructions are specifically called out for readback assurance.

choices.text: Hold short instructions, whether part of taxi instructions or a LAHSO clearance.
Every charted altitude in a published procedure.
Every traffic advisory, even after the pilot reports traffic in sight.
Every altimeter setting repeated in the ATIS broadcast.

items.question_text: What should the initial readback of a taxi, departure, or landing clearance include?
items.explanation: The note says the initial readback should include the runway assignment, including left, right, or center when applicable.

choices.text: The runway assignment, including left, right, or center when applicable.
Only the call sign and the word wilco.
The departure frequency before the runway assignment.
Only the numbers that were not published on a chart.

items.question_text: Until a pilot acknowledges a controller's clearance or instruction, what cannot the controller know?
items.explanation: The note explains that until acknowledgment, the controller cannot know whether the pilot will comply or remain as previously cleared.

choices.text: Whether the pilot will comply with the clearance or remain as previously cleared.
Whether the pilot has the destination weather aboard.
Whether the pilot can hear other aircraft on frequency.
Whether the pilot has filed an alternate airport.

items.question_text: When may a pilot be authorized to interrupt communications guard?
items.explanation: This may be authorized as necessary.

choices.text: As necessary.
Only after the pilot has declared an emergency.
Only while outside Class A airspace.
Only after switching off the assigned ATC frequency.

items.question_text: What one-radio scenario is discussed in the note to 2-4-4?
items.explanation: The note addresses a pilot with one operative communications radio who has a safety-related company problem requiring airborne communications.

choices.text: A pilot with only one operative communications radio who needs airborne company communications for a safety-related problem.
A pilot with two radios who wants to monitor guard and company simultaneously for convenience.
A pilot on the ground who wants to use company frequency before engine start.
A pilot who wants to leave guard for non-operational passenger service matters.

items.question_text: If a pilot is approved to interrupt guard under the note to 2-4-4, what should be reported to ATC?
items.explanation: The pilot informs controllers of the NAVAID voice facility and the company frequency to be monitored.

choices.text: The NAVAID voice facility and the company frequency the pilot will monitor.
Only the company's phone number and dispatch office location.
Only the estimated time away from guard.
The NAVAID frequency only, because the company frequency is not operationally relevant to ATC.

items.question_text: What messages may be transmitted under 2-4-5?
items.explanation: Only ATC-necessary or air-safety-contributing messages are authorized.

choices.text: Only messages necessary for air traffic control or otherwise contributing to air safety.
Any message that seems operationally useful to a pilot.
Any company message when the controller judges the frequency quiet enough.
Any message if the aircraft operator asks the controller to relay it.

items.question_text: If a message is not directly part of control instructions but still contributes to air safety, how does 2-4-5 treat it?
items.explanation: The paragraph expressly allows transmissions that otherwise contribute to air safety.

choices.text: It may be transmitted.
It may not be transmitted unless it is rephrased as a clearance.
It may be transmitted only after obtaining supervisory approval.
It may be transmitted only if the pilot first requests the information by call sign.

items.question_text: Which situation would violate 2-4-5 if no safety issue exists?
items.explanation: A routine non-safety company message does not meet the ATC-or-air-safety standard.

choices.text: Passing a routine gate-assignment update that does not affect air traffic control or air safety.
Passing a safety-related operational message.
Issuing a traffic-control instruction.
Relaying a message that contributes to the safe conduct of flight.

items.question_text: If deceptive or phantom transmissions are being received in an area, what must be broadcast to aircraft?
items.explanation: The rule requires an alert to aircraft operating on all frequencies within the affected area.

choices.text: An alert on all frequencies within the affected area.
A warning only on the primary local frequency.
A request for pilots to ignore all ATC instructions until further notice.
A notification to company frequencies instead of ATC frequencies.

items.question_text: Which action comes after broadcasting the alert but before notifying the operations supervisor?
items.explanation: The paragraph sequence is correct, broadcast, collect, then notify.

choices.text: Collect pertinent information regarding the incident.
Issue a blanket instruction for all aircraft to squawk standby.
Terminate radar services in the affected area.
Direct all aircraft to change to guard frequency.

items.question_text: Complete the prescribed warning phraseology: "EXERCISE EXTREME CAUTION ON ALL FREQUENCIES AND _____ INSTRUCTIONS."
items.explanation: The published example ends with "VERIFY INSTRUCTIONS."

choices.text: VERIFY

items.question_text: When relaying operational information to an aircraft or aircraft operator, what must you include?
items.explanation: The controller must give the source of the message.

choices.text: The source of the message.
The controller's operating initials.
The time the message was approved by dispatch.
The weather at the receiving aircraft's destination.

items.question_text: What standing arrangement does 2-4-7 prohibit when handling relayed operational messages?
items.explanation: Controllers are told not to agree to handle such messages on a regular basis.

choices.text: Agreeing to handle such messages on a regular basis.
Relaying official FAA messages as required.
Relaying operational information when necessary.
Identifying the source of a relayed message.

items.question_text: Which relay category is specifically authorized under 2-4-7 in addition to as-needed civil operational relays?
items.explanation: The paragraph expressly authorizes relaying official FAA messages and operational information to military aircraft on or planning to operate on IRs.

choices.text: Official FAA messages as required.
Routine airline administrative traffic whenever requested.
Passenger service information unrelated to flight operations.
Only company dispatch messages sent over a NAVAID voice facility.

items.question_text: Which sequence matches the standard format for initial radio contact with an aircraft?
items.explanation: The initial format is aircraft identification, ATC unit identification, message if any, and "over" if required.

choices.text: Aircraft identification, ATC unit identification, message if any, and "over" if required.
ATC unit identification, aircraft identification, message if any, and "over" if required.
Message first, then aircraft identification, then ATC unit identification.
Aircraft identification, message if any, ATC unit identification, and always "over."

items.question_text: On subsequent radio transmissions from the same sector or position, what identification may normally be omitted?
items.explanation: The subsequent-transmission rule keeps the same format except that the ATC unit identification may be omitted.

choices.text: The identification of the ATC unit.
The identification of the aircraft.
The message, if it is short.
The word "over" only during approach control operations.

items.question_text: During what portion of a radar approach may a terminal controller omit aircraft identification after initial contact?
items.explanation: The terminal exception in 2-4-8 allows omission of aircraft identification during the final portion of a radar approach.

choices.text: During the final portion of a radar approach.
As soon as radar contact is established.
Only after the aircraft reports the runway in sight.
Only after the aircraft is switched to tower.
